sentences.text: Prochnik.
You mean the camps and things like that?
Discussion how?
Just, whatever?
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The war sort of splits up into two energies for me.
One is my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] exciting experience of it because to be a child and growing up in New York during World War II was very exciting.
It's hard to describe now.
I mean, it's hard to understand because nothing like that has happened since.
But for a kid, it had a kind of movie-like energy to it.
You'd collect scrap metal, and you'd, you know-- and you'd buy war stamps or sell war stamps.
You know, there was a sense that the good people of the world were fighting the bad people.
It was the last war where that feeling really existed.

So that was very, very-- you know, on the war movies, you would see all these films, and you'd walk out of the theater identified with Errol Flynn or Tyrone Power or whoever else, other wonderful Jewish actors [LAUGHS] who were making the movies.
So that was-- that, that was very, very exciting.

We-- I, I used to dream of-- I think Woody Allen does that in one of his movies-- but I used to-- and as all kids my age did-- that I would be the one who would spot the Japanese plane flying over New York and become the great hero.
We used to actually get these little booklets that were handed out by Wonder Bread or one of those companies, identifying in silhouette all the enemy planes.
And you could go on the roof and be part of like the civil defense thing where you would look through binoculars for enemy planes at night.
You know, and there would be drills where the windows were covered up, you know, when they would-- and lights had to be turned off.
The whole city would go dark.
That's a very unifying kind of an experience.
That's a feeling of that something very big is happening, and you're part of it.
We would have maps up, you know, in our--
That's the American experience.
So we have the maps up on the wall with the pins showing how the Allied troops are advancing, and of they've landed in Sicily.
And they're moving here, and this is what's happening in the Pacific.
All that's very, very exciting.
Then there is the more tragic sense of the war.
That's coming through what I'm picking up primarily from my father.
Because my mother's side of the family has mostly gotten out.
I see my father's life, first of all, very dislocated.

I see him having one heart attack then another heart attack, being very, very unhappy-- not in a land where he's comfortable, not in a life that he's comfortable in, and not feeling like there are many choices for him to start again.
I have-- I have sort of memories of him writing and trying to find out about his family, and, you know, and not having success with that.
I see his humiliation in the-- in the workplace with my uncles.
That-- those are all consequences of the war, you know, which are affecting his life.
They're affecting me.
I'm, for whatever exact reasons-- partially my sensitivity and God knows what else-- I actually develop an ulcer.
I'm only 12 years old, and I get an ulcer.
Kids at 12 generally do not get ulcers.
So obviously, I've been very sensitive to what has happened.
And I'm speaking a language which I will then later make a living in as a writer and a communicator, and which I'm very good at.
But it's an imposed language.
To this day, if I speak Polish-- which I rarely do-- but if I speak Polish, my voice changes.
It actually comes down like half an octave.
It goes into a more familiar place.
And that's what I mean about how war, you know, it affects you.
It is extreme if you're sitting in a concentration camp and seems to be not affecting you if you're living in New York City.
But of course, it does.
It does.
So that-- those are the two experiences of the war.
The war ends-- very exciting, enormously exciting.
Oh, absolutely.
Unbelievable.
Well, I remember things leading up to it, too.
I remember Roosevelt dying shortly before, a tremendous impact on everybody.
People crying in the streets and all that.
But then because he's the one who's sort of leading the United States through this war of good against bad, you know.
It's only years later and find out that Roosevelt-- there were many things he could have done and didn't do for the Jews certainly.
But the war ending, yes, enormous excitement.
A whole new world is beginning.
I remember-- and then again, I can't tell you dates.
But I remember right after the war I think after Japan surrenders, there's this enor-- and this is not just VE Day.
This is like even I think a few months later.
The whole fleet is in the Hudson River.
Planes over the head.
You know, the president is here.
You know, the-- those are historic times.
I remember Roosevelt, by the way, going down Broadway in an open car with Fiorello LaGuardia, who was the mayor of New York, with Roosevelt with the famous black cape of his.
And young as I was, you know you're watching history.
You know, because they-- those were big people.
At least the made them very big.
I remember seeing Eisenhower, you know, coming back after the war and a parade for him as he, you know, rides through the streets of New York, that kind of stuff.
I remember seeing cam-- pictures of the camps in the, of course, in the movies.
Very-- I always looked at my father because he was the main barometer for the main feeling.
He would be in tears.
I mean, everybody was affected.
But he was affected because that could be his family.
And then I think the war ends, and he discovers that three of his brothers and their wives and all their children have been murdered.
One brother spent the whole war or part of the war hiding in a cemetery.
And another sister had gotten away, so you know, two of-- two of his siblings survived, but the family otherwise, decimated.
That's my-- you know, the impact of it.
I feel it.
I'm very affected by it.
But I've also created a certain kind of protective coat.
You know what I mean?
I'm an American kid in a way.
It's sort of I know it's my past, but in a way I don't want to know about it.
It's not that I block it out.
I always know about it, but there's that.
And of course, you can't protect yourself from it.
It stays with you, and it affects you.
So that's sort of the aftermath of the war.
My mother actually reconnects with my governess.
Find-- I mean, she must receive a letter from her and begins to send her packages-- food.
Now she's living in Poland.
To me, this governess, who was my mother more than my mother, I must-- without being aware of it at the time-- must have been really angry about her leaving.
You know, a child doesn't understand why somebody like that leaves you.
Because I never write to her.
I could have easily added something to a letter my mother was writing.
I'm sure my mother even probably said to me, why don't you write?
No, I never write to her, never want to know much about her.
And it's only years later when I go to my mother's house-- many years later-- and there is a photograph lying out on the table by the phone in her house.
And I look at that picture.
I don't remember what my governess looks like.
I pick up the picture and without hardly knowing--
Her name is [?
Stepha. ?] And with hardly knowing what I'm doing, I take the picture, and I kiss it.
By the time I'm in my 20s or 30s.
So you can see how deep that thing was.
But my mother reconnects with her and continues to send her things until, I guess, eventually she dies, probably of old age or whatever.
What do I do?
I go to-- I go to-- I finish with PS 87.
I go-- so we're living in Manhattan on the West Side.
We moved to 81st Street.
I go to the Bronx High School of Science-- genius school, 98% Jewish, one token black, one token Oriental kid.
And an amazing school.
I mean, I only bother to-- usually you don't mention high school.
This is the high school that you go to, and later on if you went to Harvard or MIT, it's a letdown-- unbelievable.
I think that school has five-- four or five Nobel prize winners came out of one high school.
That's the kind of school it was.
I'm not interested in science at all.
I hate science.
I hate math.
I'm a lousy student.
I hate, you know, the discipline of school.
My father has been getting basically sicker and sicker.
And in 1949 when I am 15 and 1/2, he dies of a final heart attack.
So that leaves my mother, me, and my sister.
Yeah.
I become sort of the--
She manages.
My, my uncle helps her out financially.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe my father left a bit.
I mean, she manages.
She's good with it.
I really don't know exa-- she doesn't work, you know what I mean?
I go to school.
I go through, and I finish high school.
I then go to City College.
I don't like school.
I want to be a writer.
My family, being a good Jewish family, is in despair.
[LAUGHS] A writer.
You know, a writer?
You're going to be a writer?
A writer, you know.
But, yes.
And so I go to City College.
I'm an English major.
I go for like about two and a half years, and then I drop out of school.
No.
No, no.
I, I go into films.
At that time, film seemed like the most magical thing in the world.
It didn't exist-- there were no film schools.
There were no film courses.
You know, nobody-- not every other second person was writing screenplays.
Film was in Hollywood.
I'm in New York.
But I remember one day going to hear-- I go to the Museum of Modern Art all the time with my friends.
We see all the movies, all the wonderful early German, you know, films and all the French classics and the Russian Eisenstein movies.
And that's what we talk about is movies.
And I hear a man give a lecture one time who-- about making documentary films or something.
I think, somebody gets paid for making movies?
So eventually I, I get a job working in-- when I drop out of school, I get a job working in films as a apprentice film editor.
And I work my way up, and I become a film editor.
Continue writing all the time, but I don't make my living at writing.
I, I make my living as a film editor for many years.
And I-- meanwhile I've met a girl who I marry, and we have two children, and we're raising the kids.
And I, I go-- I begin to direct.
I direct television commercials for a couple of years in New York.
And then one day through one of those wonderful flukes, I get a phone call from the David Merrick office-- big Broadway producer of his time, David Merrick.
And at that point, I'm between careers.
I'm not sure what I want to do.
I know I don't want to direct television commercials anymore.
And he asked me if I-- the offi-- the secretary asked me if I could come down there and meet with a Mr.
Logan who has read one of my scripts and would like to talk to me if I had a film.
And I go, Logan?
Logan?
Could that be Josh Logan, the famous, legendary director, you know, of and or whatever.
But I'm so cool, I didn't even ask.
I go, yeah, well, yeah, sure.
I'll come down.
It turns out to be Josh Logan.
And on the basis of a script that he's read, I'm hired to write a screenplay for a major film.
And so my writing career begins.
And you're living in New York?
Your children are growing up.
Children are growing up.
My mother is living in New York.
My aunts and uncles are still living there.
My sister, her kids.

Well, one of the things I want to say about family life, because we've been doing so much about the war, is I remember once seeing an interview with the Taviani-- is that what they're called-- brothers, the two Italian filmmakers.
And they said horrible as World War II was-- and they lived in Italy, where so many people were killed in the partisan wars and everything.
They said in a way, for all the terrors of it, it was a very vibrant time.
They said it was like an age-- I don't they use the word of gold, but one of the more precious metals.
And they said and what followed it is like the age of lead.

And while I can't make exactly that comparison in my life, there is no question that there is a vibrancy to the war and to those war years and the closeness of the family and, you know, the things-- without romanticizing, because a lot of shit is going down, but that later, of course, dissipates.
The family, and, you know, the older people begin to die off.
The family spreads out.
There isn't that cohesion anymore and that particular vibrancy of that feeling that there is a period where maybe the quality of this world is going to change.
And I do realize in retrospect that's a very narrow Western world.
Nobody's concerned about what happens to the Arabs in Algeria or the blacks in Africa.
But nevertheless, for a child, for an idealistic child, there is that feeling that a better world is coming.
And that's not the way history particularly seems to unfold.
So it's almost like-- I mean, I remember, for instance, near the end of the war going to the movies with my father.
They used to have these theaters that showed newsreels only before the days of television-- only newsreels.
There was a theater called the Newsreel Theatre.
And so they were showing this little film about how the United Nations was going to work.
I'll never forget it.
It probably exists in some archive somewhere.
Two guys on the subway, and one is completely cynical and saying, oh yeah, it's going to-- I says, yes, because if there are bad nations, the good nations will come together.
And they will see that bad nations never do anything again.
It's that kind of energy, you know?
That kind of feeling about a new world, which if you lived through that time and then see the world that follows, while some good things happen, obviously, that dream is not fulfilled.
And to this day, I have missed that.
I mean, I don't miss the war.
But I miss the fact that-- that sense of community, that sense of purpose, that sense of a defining good has eked out of our lives more and more.
Good questions.

If you had asked me in my 20s, 30s who I was, the first thing I would have said then was an artist because I had to have a real struggle to find and establish that identity in a family where no one had ever been an artist, and there was only fear and negativity about it.
So that would have been the first thing.
I had developed my own political conscience as a kid, going from somebody who refused to read books.
It's interesting for a writer.
I only would read comic books.
I was the despair of my family.
I would only read comic books.

And then one summer when I couldn't get my comic books and was forced to begin reading, I went to a library and took Jack London out of the library and went in one incredible summer from Jack London to Arthur Koestler to John Dos Passos and became a self-styled leftist, socialist, communist, but totally of my own making.
You know, really struck-- 16 years old, totally struck by the incredible injustices in the world, and how-- you know, I'd go to a movie.
And they'd show a newsreel, and say, and the latest fashions from France.
And I would sit there saying fashions from France when millions are starving?
You know, that kind of feeling.
And briefly, briefly, at that young age, I found myself moving towards the American Labor Party, which was really a quasi wing of the Communist Party under disguise.
And it wasn't so much that it was communist.
I found that early on that I did not like organizations.
I did not like organizational thinking because they all had their own orthodoxies.
And I was not-- you know, I didn't like orthodoxies.
So I formed my own political views, and I think they have-- I've remained all my life basically-- what is it called today?
A liberal.
I mean, I'm not active in any group, and yet I have strong feelings, you know, about the political state of things.
And certainly I've never lost that awareness.
So artist-- everybody I knew was basically somewhere to the left.
When I hear about kids who were Republicans, I can't-- it boggles the mind, you know, to this day.
How can a young person be a Republican?
Never have understood and never will.
So we were like that-- liberal, you know, liberal orientation, artist, American.
I mean, I married an American girl.
My children certainly are brought up, you know, I mean here in New-- here.
I keep saying here.
I live in LA, but in New York.
But there is always the European connection underneath.
It's always sort of-- that never goes away, you know?
And I think it's almost as I get older, I've come back to it, more closer to it again.

I think I told you before about the business with my mother, and it's jumping ahead in time, But it's an interesting phenomenon that many years later, as my mother needs somebody to look after her, and a group of-- and is looked after by Polish women in New York who are available and, you know, and who can be hired to work for relatively affordable amounts of money that I find myself having to speak to them in Polish, a language I haven't spoken for 30 years at that point, for all effective purposes.
And I find that I remember it, that I really understand Polish, that I speak Pol-- that I enjoy it.
So it never-- you know, what is it?
You never leave home, you never go away.
It never goes away.
So that stays underneath.
On, on the top layer, yes, very assimilated.
Religious practice because of my mother.
As long as my mother is alive, go to temple on Yom Kippur and go on Rosh Hashanah.
And there's a-- and there's Passover and, you know, and a few other things.
But my religious, formal religious thing, ends when my father dies.
That is to say I go to temple every day to say Kaddish for him for a whole year, twice a day.
It's quite an experience when you're 15 and 1/2.

But after that's over, without my father there as the sort of parental figure who would be making that go on longer, I basically-- you know, I do-- I stop going to-- I don't want go to shul on Saturdays anymore.
That all ends.
I don't keep a kosher home when I get married.
So yeah, that, that's the way it shifts away.
I mean, you know, it stays there, but the main dominant way that I live would be New York artist intellectual.
I think those things are very hard to tell exactly give proper weight to, you know, as what did what.
I think in my particular case-- because I think I was very emotionally sensitive to the dislocation of the war, probably a lot of it having to do with that loss of my governess at a critical time.
My sister was four years old, and for her, the loss of the governess, which she didn't have that strong bond that I did anyway, was not as traumatic it was for me.
So there was that dislocation, that sense of losing kind of an early Eden, which life in Poland felt like to me anyway as a child.
It was so privileged and so nice for the most part.
So I think that's always stayed with me.
I think it-- and that then-- and then choosing to become-- I mean, would I have become an artist if I had stayed in Poland?
Or would I have gone into the candy business and be traveling around Europe with boxes of Suchard chocolate?
I don't know.
It's interesting.
I don't know.
So you know, I mean, sometimes the loss of things, like that creates and makes you discover things about yourself.
You know, there's a whole part of my life I haven't talked about.
And that is that when I'm in my 40s, and I've already had a reasonably successful-- I mean, not enormously successful, a reasonably successful career in films in New York, I go to a party one night.
This is shortly after my, my first marriage has come to an end.
And there is a man there, a Swami there who looks at my palm, and he says, you have one of the most spiritual hands I have ever seen, and you're going to leave New York.
And I think, spiritual hands?
Give me a break.
Tell me I'm sexy.
Tell me I'm going to make money.
Don't tell me I'm spiritual.
And don't tell me I'm going to leave New York because I'm not going to leave New York.
A year later-- a year later, I'm in Australia giving workshops on consciousness raising.
And a year and a half later, or two years later, I move to Australia and do nothing but teach people how to enhance the quality of their lives.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where does that come from?
You know, I mean, that's, that's extraordinary.
So-- Well, where do you think that did come from?
Well, I think that has something to do with a bunch of things.

I think it has to do with both my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it has to do with something in my family past because I think that the-- my grandfather, having been a rabbi, or if he wasn't an official rabbi, he had that quality.
In other words, in family of businessmen, he was like the spiritual anchor.

So my memory of him as a child, although I hardly knew him, was him sitting there with the Talmud or whatever books, or Rashi, whatever he was looking at, studying, you know, the way Jews rock back and forth.
And it made a very definite strong impression on me as a child.
That, I think is one aspect.
But I feel that's in my past.
I think that's in the Jewish past anyway, to work with enhancing consciousness.
I think that's a very deep part of Jewish tradition which sometimes gets lost, you know, where families turn towards the-- towards the business end of life.
For me, it was very strong.
And I think maybe because of the war, I had been more sensitized to people's suffering, to, to people's dislocations, to people searching for their identities.
I think, you know, that plus the unexplainable aspect of it, but I think this has definitely contributed to it.
So here I'm in my mid-forties.
I totally change my life.
Give up films, everybody thinks I'm crazy, and I move to Australia.
I move to Australia.
I live there fore six years, and all I do is teach people how to make the most of their lives, how to come to a deeper sense of understanding themselves.
There's another journey for you.
And at the end of that time--
End of that time I met my present wife, who I meet in Australia.
She's Belgian.
She's traveling through Australia when we meet.
She [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] joins me in the work I'm doing.
We leave Australia eventually.
We come back to the states, live in New York for about two years.
And then we move out here to Los Angeles where I-- I come out here to write a film for Richard Dreyfuss, who I've known from New York days, and have been living here ever since.
Primarily writing, occasionally still doing workshops, rarely.
You never lose-- I'm sure I'll go back to it again.
You know, it's like Shane when he hangs up his guns.
[LAUGHS] You never stop being a teacher anyway.
And my writing is about discovering yourself anyway.
Everything I write is about that in one form or another.
Yeah, that's a very good question.
I think that's been happening for me very strongly in the last few years.
I never-- I don't feel it's a process that completes itself.
It's one of the things I used to teach people is that there is no final place you get to and that where you sit in like some perfect sauna or something.
You know, you just sit back and relax.
I mean, it's always the journey continues till the moment you stop breathing.
But yes, I would say that sense of appreciation for my past, of a deeper sense of the integration of the coming out of hiding with some of the wounds that I experienced from the war, yes, definitely.
I feel this is the era now when that is happening for me.
And so it's a kind of interesting time that we should be doing this.
It's a very perfect time for us to be doing this interview because this is a time when I feel it's, it's really in the process of happening.
How so with family?
That's a-- that's hard for me to answer.
That's hard.
That's not quite a question.
I mean, you mean like my [? whole ?] family, my past family, my present family?
It's a--
I think you'd have to take a whole new roll of tape, and we'd have to do more.
And I think that's-- that's maybe for another time.
